reviewer.displayName: VSBDev — academic reviewer via Claude Code

evidenceChecked: references.json#source-authoring-evidence (candidate-pinned synthesis, executive conclusion, sections 4-9 and 12, and evidence-to-rule matrix)
references.json#source-adesope-et-al-2017 (Crossref DOI record and deposited abstract)
references.json#source-crissman-2006 (University of Nebraska-Lincoln abstract and citation record)
references.json#source-tetzlaff-et-al-2025 (peDOCS abstract record and Crossref DOI metadata)

findings.finding: The glossary aliases retention horizon to retention interval, but the cited distributed-practice literature uses retention interval for the post-practice interval before the final test. In the worked example the planning horizon is fourteen days while the interval after the day-13 opportunity is one day. No numerical gap claim is made, so this terminology mismatch does not invalidate the plan, but it could make a learner misread the cited gap-to-interval relation.
findings.resolution: Remove the alias or distinguish the whole planning horizon from the post-practice retention interval in the glossary and content/030-concept.md#definition-spacing.

findings.finding: The stimulus says both models are already taught and their selection conditions are confused, a case for which the verified interleaving evidence supports immediate discrimination practice. The common-errors list warns against mixing unfamiliar models and the acceptable-responses list expressly affirms only a blocked-then-interleaved route. Although the warning is correct for material without a usable initial representation, its placement here could lead a scorer to penalize a source-consistent immediate interleaving plan.
findings.resolution: Qualify the common error as applying when a usable initial representation is absent, and add that immediate discrimination practice is acceptable for the already-taught confusable models in this stimulus.

findings.finding: The machine-readable title calls the assessment a mastery gate without the provisional qualifier used in the learner-facing scene and glossary. Because this lesson defines mastery as evidence after delay and variation, an immediate end-of-lesson assessment alone cannot establish durable mastery; indexes or exports may surface the title apart from the qualifying prose.
findings.resolution: Retitle the artifact as a provisional mastery gate or use another title that preserves the lesson's bounded mastery definition.

findings.finding: All six material claims are supported at or below their declared scope and confidence. The direct and meta-analytic records verify the retrieval, spacing, feedback, interleaving, worked-example, and adaptive-assistance propositions; the pack explicitly labels the six-part loop, exact schedules, practice ladder, decision rules, and point threshold as EmbeddedKnowledge-authored conventions rather than empirical laws.
findings.resolution: No change required for academic approval. Preserve the existing scope and uncertainty boundaries in future revisions.

findings.finding: Independent recalculation found internally correct quantitative and answer logic: scene minutes sum to 115, item points sum to 20, every rubric total matches its item, the 16-of-20 gate with 4-of-6 constructed-response floors is attainable and non-vacuous, and all worked, practice, transfer, and assessment timelines are consistent. No item rewards a scientifically false conclusion once the bounded answer-key qualification above is applied.
findings.resolution: No change required for academic approval.

limitations: The SAGE publisher pages for the Roediger-Karpicke and Adesope records returned HTTP 403. Their bibliographic and abstract-level claims were verified through authoritative PubMed and Crossref records without bypassing access controls.

The evidence base is heterogeneous and includes verbal-recall, mathematics, and other structured learning domains; the pack's scope and uncertainty fields appropriately disclose its bounded generalization to foundation-level academic planning.
This review evaluates academic correctness and evidence only; it does not claim learner-outcome validation or perform another quorum role.
